planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or take stem cuttings in summer. Smooth phlox grows naturally in moist, open meadows, prairie edges, and streambank margins and tolerates partial shade better than many native phloxes.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; smooth phlox is a tall, erect native perennial distinguished from other tall phloxes by its notably smooth, glossy, dark green leaves — a trait reflected in both the common name and the species epithet (glaberrima means 'most glabrous' or smoothest). Large clusters of brilliant magenta-pink to lavender flowers bloom in early summer, slightly earlier than garden phlox (P. paniculata), attracting hummingbirds, swallowtail butterflies, and sphinx moths. It grows naturally in the moist meadows and prairie margins of the eastern and central US, thriving in the humid heat of the Southeast where P. paniculata often suffers from powdery mildew. More resistant to foliar diseases than the European garden phlox cultivars. An excellent alternative to garden phlox for zone 9 gardens seeking a heat-tough native with equally spectacular flower display.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in average to fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; cutleaf ground cherry self-sows prolifically and often naturalizes in warm gardens. In zone 9, it may grow as a self-sowing perennial annual, emerging from seed each spring.

directions: Direct sow in average, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; cutleaf ground cherry is a tropical annual widely distributed throughout warm regions of the Americas, distinguished from other ground cherries by its more deeply lobed, angular leaves that give the plant a distinctive, fernlike texture among the lantern-papery husked physalis clan. The small yellow berries with papery, lantern-shaped husks ripen inside the husk from green to yellow and have a sweet, tropical flavor suitable for eating raw, making jam, or drying. Widely used as a traditional food plant in Mexico and Central America, where it grows as a weed of agricultural fields and gardens. Very drought-tolerant once established and naturalistic in garden settings where it self-sows to maintain a permanent colony. Distinguished from the toxic black nightshade (Solanum) relatives by the distinctive papery husk enclosing each fruit.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in well-drained to dry, alkaline to slightly acidic, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; fourwing saltbush tolerates heavy clay, poor soils, and moderate salinity. It is dioecious — plant male and female shrubs for fruit set.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; fourwing saltbush is one of the most ecologically important and widely distributed native shrubs of the arid and semi-arid West and Southwest, occurring naturally from Oregon to Texas and from the Plains to the Pacific Coast in desert shrubland, grasslands, and disturbed alkaline soils. The distinctive four-winged seed capsules that cover female shrubs in fall are immediately recognizable and are an important food source for pronghorn antelope, mule deer, and many birds. The gray-green, slightly mealy foliage is moderately salt-tolerant, making it valuable in alkaline soils and roadsides with ice melt. Native American tribes used the seeds as a grain and the leaves as a salt substitute. The plant is one of the primary components of native range reclamation seedings in the West and an outstanding drought-adapted landscape shrub for dry, alkaline, or sandy conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, rocky, limestone-derived, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; big red sage grows naturally on rocky limestone outcrops and cliff faces of the Edwards Plateau in central Texas and is adapted to dry, thin, calcareous soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry, rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; big red sage is a rare and distinctive Texas endemic of the Edwards Plateau, where it grows on exposed limestone outcrops, ledges, and thin, rocky calcareous soils in areas of central Texas. The flowers are among the largest of any native Salvia — rich scarlet-crimson tubes nearly 1.5 inches long on tall, erect spikes — making it one of the most spectacular fall-blooming salvias in cultivation and an exceptional hummingbird attractor. The common name 'penstemonoides' (penstemon-like) refers to the form of the flower, which somewhat resembles a penstemon in shape. A rare plant in the wild with a limited natural range, it has gained popularity in native plant horticulture as a stunning, drought-tolerant hummingbird and butterfly plant for zone 7–10 dry gardens. Thrives in alkaline limestone soils typical of the Texas Hill Country.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, lean, rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; shrubby blue sage is a woody, somewhat sprawling shrub native to the Rio Grande Plain and Trans-Pecos Texas, tolerating alkaline limestone soils, extreme heat, and extended drought.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, rocky or sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun in zones 8–11; shrubby blue sage is a woody native salvia of southern Texas and northern Mexico, producing bright lavender-blue flowers in loose terminal spikes over a very long season — from early spring to hard frost in warm climates — making it one of the most persistently flowering native sages in the South Texas garden. The strongly aromatic, gray-green, woolly-felted leaves have a distinctive strong fragrance and have been used traditionally in Mexico as a tea herb (mejorana is a traditional name in northern Mexico). The shrubby, semi-evergreen habit and extreme drought tolerance make it valuable as a foundation plant or informal hedge in alkaline, dry landscape conditions where few shrubs thrive. Consistently attractive to migrating hummingbirds, monarch butterflies, and native bees throughout its long blooming season.
